summary: Gruber dismantles a Yahoo Finance pundit's claim that 'it's over for Apple,' calling it unhinged from reality. Hilary Kramer argued Apple is doomed as a hardware company, citing dropping subscription prices, youth preference for Android, and comparing Apple to Wang Laboratories. Gruber methodically debunks each claim: Apple didn't drop subscription prices, its Services revenue grew 88% over three years, 83% of U.S. teens own iPhones, and Microsoft Stores are ghost towns. In a footnote, he offers a nuanced take on Apple as a 'hardware company,' arguing the real story is the fusion of hardware and software that creates the product experience.
key_points: Kramer's claim that Apple dropped subscription prices is factually wrong — Apple had never previously announced prices for Arcade or TV+
Survey data shows 83% of U.S. teens own iPhones and 86% expect their next phone to be an iPhone, directly contradicting the claim that youth prefer Android
Comparing Apple to Wang Laboratories — a company that ceased functioning before any current teenager was born — reveals how disconnected the analysis is
Apple is financially a hardware company, but functionally it succeeds because of its software and the fusion between hardware and software
money_quotes: This rant is insane. It's not just a bad opinion, it's unhinged from reality.
Wang? Wang? None of the 12-25 year-olds she goes on to talk about have ever been alive while Wang was a functioning company.
The implication here isn't that Apple is headed for a drop but that Apple is headed out of business. It's been a long while since I've seen that one.
Kramer would be more accurate if she got on TV and claimed that what teenagers want is more homework and longer school days.
And Microsoft Stores? They're ghost towns. You don't have to be a research analyst to walk past stores and see how crowded they are.
Apple's software is the whole reason people want to buy Apple hardware.

If you want to understand the company, if you want to understand why Apple has an ever-growing number of customers, you have to look not just at software, too, but at the fusion between hardware and software — the way Apple creates an entire product experience.
key_insight: Confident predictions of Apple's demise are almost always built on claims that are trivially disprovable with publicly available data, revealing more about the pundit's detachment from reality than about Apple's prospects.
